jokes.punchline: Jerry's analogy — Noreen listened to Elaine like Mrs. Costanza listened to the 'Chinese' — and then the fortune cookie observation: the Chinese have institutionalized the giving of advice and everyone accepts it.
jokes.explanation: The fortune cookie line ties together the episode's thematic thread about cultural authority. 'Everybody listens to the Chinese' as a statement of social fact — because of fortune cookies — is an absurdist but logical observation in the Seinfeld universe. Good structural closure.

jokes.text: You couldn't get away with that at other restaurants. Yeah, no one's reading any rolled-up messages in a knish.
jokes.setup: Jerry riffs on why fortune cookies work as a delivery mechanism for wisdom.
jokes.punchline: 'No one's reading any rolled-up messages in a knish' — the specific Jewish food parallel is perfect: the knish as an imagined vehicle for fortunes, and why it would fail.
jokes.explanation: 'No one's reading any rolled-up messages in a knish' is an exceptional line. The knish as the Jewish analogue to the fortune cookie is an inspired parallel — it's the right ethnic comparison given the episode's themes, it's a vivid specific image, and the phrase 'rolled-up messages in a knish' is inherently funny. This is the level of specific observational comedy that defines Seinfeld at its best.

jokes.text: Well, it had to happen. I knew it. I predicted it. / Saw both of them today. What a disaster. / I'm running all over Queens. First, I saw my mother. We had lunch together. I never had lunch with my mother before. It's like a date.
jokes.setup: George reflects on his day navigating his newly separated parents.
jokes.punchline: 'I never had lunch with my mother before. It's like a date' — George's inability to have normal maternal interactions, framing a lunch with his mother as a novel romantic experience.
jokes.explanation: 'It's like a date' — George describing lunch with his mother as a romantic experience captures both his emotional isolation and the strangeness of his family dynamics. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: We played Clue. / All day with this!
jokes.setup: George describes his day — lunch with his mother, then driving to Kew Gardens to see his father.
jokes.punchline: The visit with his father consisted of playing Clue — an absurdly mundane board game choice for a tense family crisis day. 'All day with this!' is the exhausted button.
jokes.explanation: 'We played Clue' as the culminating activity of a day of family crisis is perfectly bathetic. The specificity of the board game choice (Clue, not Monopoly, not cards) and 'All day with this!' as George's exhausted summary is good character comedy with excellent economy.

jokes.text: Hey, Jerry! Guess what? The Kramer name might live on! Noreen's late! She's late!
jokes.setup: Kramer bursts in with dramatic news about Noreen.
jokes.punchline: Kramer has potentially impregnated Noreen — the woman he just met this episode, having inserted himself into her life as her protector/advisor — completing the fertility subplot in the most absurd way possible.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best structural joke and a near-perfect Seinfeld ending. The fertility subplot (low sperm count → go commando → meet Noreen → send her to army) converges with the Noreen subplot (Elaine ruins her relationships → Kramer takes over) in the most gloriously irresponsible way. 'The Kramer name might live on!' pays off the extinction callback from earlier. The structure is exemplary: multiple storylines collide in a single announcement that is simultaneously the episode's darkest and funniest beat. 'She's late! She's late!' with repeated emphasis is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Who are you? / I'm Frank Costanza's lawyer.
jokes.setup: The episode's final beat — someone encounters an unknown person.
jokes.punchline: The final reveal confirms the cape lawyer in person (presumably in the cape). The callback to the episode-long cape mystery as the very last moment is a perfect button.
jokes.explanation: The cold button ending — the cape lawyer showing up and identifying himself — is structurally perfect. The episode spent considerable time on 'who is the man in the cape?' and the answer (Frank's lawyer) plus the implication that he's still wearing the cape is a great visual payoff. Simple, economical, and satisfying.

tmdb_overview: Jerry makes a blind date; Kramer makes a change to increase his sperm count.
insight: Escalation-driven episode hits 67 jokes in 22 minutes despite middling 77 score.
